content.full_text:
RAVI MEHTA
CHAIRMAN

FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION

April 3, 1997

Mr. Thomas W. Hiltachk
Bell, McAndrews & Hiltachk
455 Capitol Mall, Suite 801
Sacramento, California 95814

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. I-97-129

Dear Mr. Hiltachk:

This letter is a response to your request for advice regarding the provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

QUESTION

Does section 85305(e) allow a committee to accept contributions for the payment of a Commission fine for various campaign reporting violations occurring in 1992 through 1996, in any amount because there was no contribution limit in place during that time?

CONCLUSION

No. The limits of section 85301 apply to contributions raised to pay a fine imposed in 1997 that arises from campaign reporting violations that occurred before the effective date of Proposition 208.

FACTS

You serve as attorney and treasurer to a committee that has been fined by the Commission (after the effective date of Proposition 208) for various campaign reporting violations occurring in 1992 through 1996, prior to your retention.

¹ Government Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109 - 18997 of the California Code of Regulations. Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice. (Section 83114; Regulation 18239(c)(3).)

428 J STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814 (916) 322-5660

File No. I-97-129
Page 2

ANALYSIS

Proposition 208 imposes fundraising blackout periods on candidates. Under section
85305, candidates may not accept contributions until six months before an upcoming election in
districts of fewer than a million residents, and twelve months before an upcoming election in
districts of a million or more residents. (Section 85305(a) and (b).) Once the fundraising
window for a particular election opens, a candidate may continue to raise funds until 90 days
following his or her withdrawal, defeat, or election to office. However, the candidate may use
funds collected in the immediate post-election period only to pay outstanding campaign debt.
(Section 85305(c).)

Regardless of the fundraising blackout periods, Proposition 208 permits candidates to
raise funds at any time for the limited purposes of retiring debts from elections that took place
before January 1, 1997, and paying certain legal expenses. (Section 85305(c) and (d).) Section
85305(c)-(e) provides as follows:

"(c) No candidate or the controlled committee of such
candidate shall accept contributions more than 90 days after the
date of withdrawal, defeat, or election to office. Contributions
accepted immediately following such an election or withdrawal
and up to 90 days after that date shall be used only to pay
outstanding bills or debts owed by the candidate or controlled
committee. This section shall not apply to retiring debts incurred
with respect to any election held prior to the effective date of this
act, provided such funds are collected pursuant to the contribution
limits specified in Article 3 (commencing with Section 85300) of
this act, applied separately for each prior election for which debts
are being retired, and such funds raised shall not count against the
contribution limitations applicable for any election following the
effective date of this act.

(d) Notwithstanding subdivision (c), funds may be
collected at any time to pay for attorney's fees for litigation or
administrative action which arises directly out of a candidate's or
elected officer's alleged violation of state or local campaign,
disclosure, or election laws or for a fine or assessment imposed by
any governmental agency for violations of this act or this title, or
for a recount or contest of the validity of an election, or for any
expense directly associated with an external audit or unresolved tax
liability of the campaign by the candidate or the candidate's
controlled committee; provided such funds are collected pursuant
to the contribution limits of this act.

File No. I-97-129
Page 3

(e) Contributions pursuant to subdivisions (c) and (d) of
this provision shall be considered contributions raised for the
election in which the debts, fines, assessments, recounts, contests,
audits, or tax liabilities were incurred and shall be subject to the
contribution limits of that election." (Emphasis added.)

Section 85305(c) permits a candidate to collect funds at any time (including during the
blackout period) to repay old debt, provided the candidate collects the funds subject to the
contribution limits specified in article 3 of the Act. Section 85305(d) similarly permits a
candidate to collect funds at any time (including during the blackout period) to pay a fine
imposed by the Commission, provided the candidate collects the funds pursuant to the
contribution limits of the Act. Thus, subdivisions (c) and (d) set forth the general rule that
candidates may collect funds at any time to repay old debt or to pay enumerated legal expenses,
as long as the candidates collect the funds subject to the applicable contribution limit imposed by
Proposition 208.

We interpret section 85305(e) to be aimed at preventing evasion of Proposition 208's
contribution limits for funds raised following an election that is held after the effective date,
under the limits of Proposition 208.² We do not interpret section 85305(e) as applicable to funds
raised to pay down debt or fines incurred prior to the effective date, with the result that
candidates could accept unlimited contributions for such debt or fines because there were no
contribution limits in effect in pre-208 elections. Such an interpretation would render
meaningless the language of section 85305(c) stating that funds may be raised to retire pre-
effective date debt "provided such funds are collected pursuant to the contribution limits in
Article 3 ..." and the language of section 85305(d) stating that funds may be collected for
enumerated legal expenses "provided such funds are collected pursuant to the contribution limits
of this act ...."

The language of section 85305(e) may be somewhat ambiguous. While Proposition 208
expressly addressed the repayment of debt incurred prior to the effective date, it did not
specifically address the collection of funds during the blackout period to pay a fine for violations
that occurred before the effective date. Nevertheless, we interpret section 85305(d) to mean that
funds collected during the blackout period to pay fines for pre-effective date violations are

² Section 85305(e) directs the candidate to the applicable contribution limit under Proposition 208.
Subdivision (e) provides that contributions that a candidate collects in the immediate post-election period to pay
outstanding debts (under section 85305(c)), and contributions that a candidate collects at any time to pay for
enumerated legal expenses (under section 85305(d)), are subject to the same contribution limit as the election that
the debt or legal expense relates to.

File No. I-97-129
Page 4

subject to the contribution limits set forth in article 3. This interpretation is in accord with the
intent of Proposition 208 to minimize the appearance of corruption caused by large contributors
and is analogous to Proposition 208's treatment of pre-effective date debt.

If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-
5660.

Sincerely,

Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel

By: Hyla P. Wagner
Staff Counsel, Legal Division

SGC:HPW:ak

Does section 85305(e) allow a committee to accept contributions for the payment of a

No. The limits of section 85301 apply to contributions raised to pay a fine imposed in
1997 that arises from campaign reporting violations that occurred before the effective date of
Proposition 208.

QUESTION: Does section 85305(e) allow a committee to accept contributions for the payment of a
Commission fine for various campaign reporting violations occurring in 1992 through 1996, in
any amount because there was no contribution limit in place during that time?

CONCLUSION: No. The limits of section 85301 apply to contributions raised to pay a fine imposed in
1997 that arises from campaign reporting violations that occurred before the effective date of
Proposition 208.
